It's hard for me to posit from above Windows-based failure that the above indicated misplacement. Can I ask if the following is the correct reasoning, reasoning within Python 3.6?

- Calling `PyEval_InitThreads()` in `get_internals()` via nominal usage did nothing per [3.6 docs](https://docs.python.org/3.6/c-api/init.html#c.PyEval_InitThreads) stating that "This is a no-op when called for a second time", so this is a harmless (but noisy) misplacement.
- The missing call in `embed.h` are why Windows (and others?) fail. This smells like it'd happen when the embedded interpreter exits before `get_internals()` was called. Is this the case? (is it spurious, or deterministic?)

Sorry for the wait. I finally got around to reading this, and overall I really like the feature, and I'd love to include it! The main issue I have, which I sort of alluded to earlier, is the interface. Since in general there can be many list properties, I think we need a way to be able to specify lengths on a per-property basis. If users are asserting that a list property has a specific length, then they must have some foreknowledge of the data, so I think it's not unreasonable to expect them to make their intent fairly explicit. I see this as an "expert" feature anyway, so I'm not too worried about the syntax being a little verbose, as long as it can get the job done.

The simplest thing I could come up with, which seems intuitive enough, is for the user to supply the mapping
```
{element: {property: length}}
```
which in usage would look like
```python
plydata = PlyData.read('tet_binary.ply', known_list_len={'face': {'vertex_indices': 3}})
```

I think I should have some time over the next week to work on this, but if you want to take the initiative, I won't stop you!

> Does rust support utf-8 in source code?

Yep! Unicode is permitted in source code files, and moreover it is
[permitted in identifiers][play]:

```rust
let 유니코드 = true;
assert!(유니코드);
```

[play]: https://play.rust-lang.org/?version=nightly&mode=debug&edition=2018&gist=132fb2ac089e96cc383164029dc8d0cd

> Can I write a macro that aliases `clap` to 👏? :D

Not quite. You can use ~arbitrary Unicode in string literals, comments,
etc., but identifiers must meet the [Unicode identifier contraints][s].
This includes basically “things that appear in words in human
languages”, but does not include emoji.

…at least, not easily. You might be able to wrangle a procedural macro
to do it. (Simple Rust macros are term-rewriting rules: “pattern match
on this structure and emit that one”. Procedural macros are a more
powerful form of macro that execute arbitrary Rust code to transform one
token tree into another. So as long as your 👏s can be tokenized, you
might have a shot.)

[s]: http://www.unicode.org/reports/tr31/#Default_Identifier_Syntax

Thanks, have a few comments:

The main place where paths are stored now is `StorageContext`. I've actually had a TODO about how this handles windows and relative paths, so would be a good time to fix it up.

StorageContext takes in the exact user `storage_path` that's passed, as well as an optional `storage_filesystem`. Here's what I was imagining the path handling could look like:

```python
class StorageContext:
    def __init__(self, storage_path: Tuple[str, os.PathLike], storage_filesystem: Optional[pyarrow.fs.FileSystem]):
        if storage_filesystem provided:
            # just convert storage_path to posix path (don't convert to an absolute path)
            # this is because the path should stay relative to the custom_fs
        elif local path (scheme == file or no scheme):
            # expand and convert to *absolute* posix path
        else: # do nothing for URIs, which will get handled by pyarrow.
            assert is_uri(storage_path)
```

I wanted to avoid checking `is_local_path` in this class, but it seems like it's necessary to support relative / windows paths, since pyarrow will raise an error from the input to `pyarrow.fs.from_uri`.

> #EXT-X-ENDLIST

See [`--player-no-close`](https://streamlink.github.io/cli.html#cmdoption-player-no-close).

[Once the stream has been fully read by Streamlink and the ringbuffer been drained](https://github.com/streamlink/streamlink/blob/54136f414cf7c8f5054990d45844af0ee8dd6064/src/streamlink_cli/main.py#L359-L373), Streamlink CLI will close the player process and terminate by default. [WIth `--player-no-close`, it'll wait until the player process terminates (e.g. when the user closes it or when it closes on its own)](https://github.com/streamlink/streamlink/blob/54136f414cf7c8f5054990d45844af0ee8dd6064/src/streamlink_cli/output/player.py#L294-L305).

I would have expected CI to fail, but it [succeeded](https://github.com/docker/docker-py/runs/2280319072#step:4:62) because CI installs `six` due to a bunch of transitive dependencies in `test-requirements.txt` and `requirements.txt`[1].

I suggest this PR gets some improved tests to ensure this kind of issue doesn't reoccur.

One way to fix this is to pick up [this work in progress](https://github.com/docker/docker-py/commit/71411f5596dd0b3ef523af3b0af4c81e2827cc04).  I may give that a try if that's the preferred direction.

Alternatively, since `setup.py` indicates `websocket-client >= 0.32.0` but `requirements.txt` requires `websocket-client==0.56.0` perhaps `websocket-client >= 0.32.0, < 1` in `setup.py` is another way to solve this problem.  Not my favourite approach, but keeps `install_requires` pretty minimal.

Open to feedback on the preferred approach, especially from the maintainers.

1: `pytest-cov==2.6.1` and `pytest-timeout==1.3.3` in `test-requirements.txt`, as well as `websocket-client==0.56.0`, `paramiko==2.4.2`, `packaging==16.8`, and `pyOpenSSL==18.0.0` in `requirements.txt`

Let's try to reduce the amount of code duplication between actor and non-actor tasks so it is clear which parts are actor- or task-specific.

Also, I noticed you added a virtual class for SchedulingQueue but it doesn't actually get used anywhere right now. Originally I suggested doing that so you could use the same queue interface for both actor and non-actor tasks. For example, you could enqueue tasks by getting a SchedulingQueue pointer to the right queue, either an actor queue or the normal task queue, then calling `Add`. This change works fine, but does not completely resolve issue #688.

If the input batch is not an object type, such as:
```
(Pdb) x
array([[ 0.0000000e+00, -3.0517578e-05,  3.0517578e-05, ...,
        -1.2207031e-04, -1.2207031e-04, -9.1552734e-05]], dtype=float32)
(Pdb) x.shape, x.dtype
((1, 238400), dtype('float32'))
```

The estimator has no trouble running predict and loss_gradient. However, loss_gradient will always return an object type:
```
(Pdb) grad = estimator.loss_gradient(x)
(Pdb) grad
array([array([0., 0., 0., ..., 0., 0., 0.], dtype=float32)], dtype=object)
```
This will cause FGSM to fail here:
https://github.com/Trusted-AI/adversarial-robustness-toolbox/blob/dev_1.4.2/art/attacks/evasion/fast_gradient.py#L302-L307
In particular, `batch` will be of type float32 but `grad` will be of type object. Then it will return the else branch and return this error:
```
*** ValueError: The truth value of an array with more than one element is ambiguous. Use a.any() or a.all()
```
when it hits this line:
```
https://github.com/Trusted-AI/adversarial-robustness-toolbox/blob/dev_1.4.2/art/attacks/evasion/fast_gradient.py#L287
```

Instead of modifying FGSM and the other attacks, I recommend forcing the estimator to make the gradient dtype match the input to loss_gradient. This could be done by adding the following lines to the end of `loss_gradient` right before `return results`:
```
if x.dtype != np.object:
    results = np.array([i for i in results], x.dtype)
    assert results.shape == x.shape and results.dtype == x.dtype  # if you want to check
```
I approve as long as the change has been tested on the insturment (including the verbose mode :) )

> The main reason is that if multiple radios are running on the same chip (e.g. bluetooth), we probably don't want the whole chip to be reset when the Thread host starts, resetting only the needed part by software is more graceful. This increases complexity though.

Thanks @jinran-google. It makes sense. Overall the code LGTM. I just added few smaller suggestions below.

But thinking more about this, I have an idea/suggestion for a rather different overall approach which I'd like to bring up.

- I understand the main goal/reason that we don't want to reset the entire chip if there are multiple radio (BLE, etc) on it when Thread starts.
- The solution in this PR is to add mechnasim to just reset the radio.
- But we are leaving all other platform modules that OT may use and the `otInstance` itself to continue to run as before (e.g. `Alarm/Timer` platform, or UART, etc.).  I guess this is fine and works for RCP (for now).

What we really want is that when we get a reset command (from host) to not reset the whole chip. We really want to have all the platform modules reset and then reinit the `otInstance` (clear all RAM variable) and start running `otInstance` fresh.

I think we can realize this differently:
- From `Instance::Reset()` we call `otPlatReset()` which we currently assume would reset the whole chip.
- We can change the implementation of `otPlatReset()` in platform layer to not trigger chip reset, but instead it can reset all the plaform modules (Radio, alaram, etc). 
- In OT code, we change the `Instance::Reset()` such that after calling `otPlatReset()` we call `Finalize()` immediately followed by `InitSingle()` call again (to reinit the `otInstance`). 
  - If the `otPlatReset()` does actually reset the chip, then we won't get to the `Finalize()` and `InitSingle()` callls (which is fine).
  - If the `otPlatReset()` resets all the platform components/modules, then we are reinitializing the `otInstance` to start fresh (which clears all RAM variables). 

I think this can work. The main advantages I see is that it resets all components without resetting the chip. It delegates the reseting of platform modules to the platform implementation itself which can reset all platform modules (radio, timer, any other platform module we may use in future)... It avoids the need for us to add mechanism in OT To handle reset of one platform components (like reseting radio only - which can be tricky to do).

Thoughts?

`UrlDispatcher._resources` could be modified from `List[AbstractResource]` to `List[Union[AbstractResource, _PlainResourceGroup]]`.

The `_PlainResourceGroup` is a hidden container for `PlainResource` mapping that is never exposed to end user. It can have `_PlainResourceGroup.resolve()` and `_PlainResourceGroup.freeze()` methods but have no other `AbstractResource`'s methods and properties.

`UrlDispatcher.resources`, `UrlDispatcher.routes`, `UrlDispatcher.register_resource`, and `UrlDispatcher.add_resource` can be modified to work with `_PlainResourceGroup` directly if needed.

i think removing this line here will have the same effect: https://github.com/localstack/localstack/blob/61a1bbcb9a70be6d4ced28fc979ea68654c02acf/Dockerfile#L155

* make install does a `pip install -e`, which creates in the virtual environment an egg-link file to the project itself
* `pip uninstall localstack` removes that egg link (i have no idea why we/I introduced this line)
* when we then run `pip install --upgrade localstack-ext`, localstack is resolved again because it is no longer resolved

if we remove the `pip uninstall localstack` line, then:
* `pip install --upgrade localstack-ext` will happily find the localstack requirement to be satisfied (the egg-link created by `pip install -e`) and will no longer overwrite localstack

Wow, @DaelonSuzuka , that's indeed a lot of work—thanks! Its a sufficiently big extension of QtPy (with many new modules, new errors, and other changes) that I think it makes sense to ship it in 1.2, assuming @dalthviz agrees.

I highly recommend applying my GitHub suggestions first, which are generally straightforward and uncontroversial, before addressing the higher level comments and feedback, assuming you don't disagree with them; otherwise, they are likely to get outdated/lost.

Some high-level feedback and suggestions:

* [x] Right now, since QtPy is still using the implicit ("flat") package layout rather than the now-recommended explicit `src` layout, this means that `module_report` is a top-level import package equal to `qtpy` itself and will be included in the distribution package and installed in the user's environment as a separate import package, which is of course not what we want. We could exclude it with some futzing in the setuptools configuration, but IMO its still confusing to have two top-level import packages, one of which is just a development-focused helper tool. 

  Therefore, after consultation with you and @dalthviz , I suggest we do one of the following (there is a third option, making it part of the `qtpy` package itself, but I don't think that is more compelling than either of the other two for now):
  
  * Stick `module_report` inside a `tools` directory (which wouldn't be importable and is ignored by default in the new Setuptools 61 auto-discovery) and run it from there when needed *(simplest solution to the immediate problem)*
   * Make `module_report` its own separate repo/distribution package (Better usability for us and others without bundling it in QtPy itself, and if you want to help maintain it, you could much more easily be added as a Collaborator on that repo, but is more initial work, more overhead and less directly coupled to QtPy's own development)

  Making it a separate repo, installable (at least) from GitHub, is the "nicest" solution, but also the most work, up front and (to some extent) over time, so whether this is viable depends on whether you want to help maintain it, and also on if it more generally useful to application developers rather than just development of QtPy itself. So IMO, just keeping it in a `tools` directory is probably the best approach for now unless and until those other conditions are a compelling enough reason to do otherwise (which we could always do in the future). 

* [x] If we're going to check the output files into the repo, we should run `module_report` in our CIs to catch any changes and either update the files or take other action accordingly. However, given the results can vary between OSes (e.g. `DBus` on Windows) and distributions (`pip`/`conda`), it might be better to just not check them in directly, or if so make the checked-in files specifically for comparison to a per-platform CI check that determines if the output matches the expected (but that latter part would be *optional* for this PR). This could also help automate and decouple the testing from the details of the implementation, which you brought up some valid concerns about—but again, that could go in a future PR.

* [x] Instead of making all the exception messages base `PythonQtError`s (such that there's no way for tools to programmatically determine whether the module was not installed, not supported on that binding, or some other error occurred) and writing manual messages for each one (which is way more work to add, check and change, and easier to make a typo on), we should subclass it with specific errors of each type, that take a `name` and a `binding` argument (with a `msg` override) and construct the message from there. This also allows us to have it inherit the built-in `ModuleNotFoundError` so it behaves as expected.
  
    Therefore, I suggest adding the following to the `__init__.py` under the existing errors and using them accordingly:

    ```python
     class QtModuleNotFoundError(ModuleNotFoundError, PythonQtError):
        """Raised when a Python Qt binding submodule is not installed/supported."""
        _msg = 'The {name} module was not found for {binding}.'
        _msg_extra = ''

        def __init__(self, *, name, binding=None, msg=None, **msg_kwargs):
            self.binding = binding
            msg = msg or f'{self._msg} {self._msg_extra}'.strip()
            msg = msg.format(name=name, binding=binding, **msg_kwargs)
            super().__init__(msg, name=name)

    class QtBindingMissingModuleError(QtModuleNotFoundError):
        """Raised when a module is not supported by a given binding."""
        _msg_extra = 'It is not currently implemented in {binding}.'

    class QtModuleNotInstalledError(QtModuleNotFoundError):
        """Raise when a module is supported by the binding, but not installed."""
        _msg_extra = 'It must be installed separately'

        def __init__(self, *, missing_package=None, **superclass_kwargs):
            self.missing_package = missing_package
            if missing_package is not None:
                 self._msg_extra += ' as {missing_package}.'
            super().__init__(missing_package=missing_package, **superclass_kwargs)
    ```

Also, a few smaller but common issues (which I made suggestions to fix, except where noted):

* [x] Let's please not use `*` imports in `module_report`, as they are considered a very bad practice and make the code hard to understand, introspect and lint, among other reasons. It is true that QtPy uses them internally, but for a very specific purpose—ensuring it picks up the full namespace of the packages it wraps without hardcoding—that isn't the case here. Also, we should use standard, conventional PEP 8 import ordering.
* [x] Module-level constants are ALL_CAPS per PEP 8 and standard convention
* [x] Don't forget to add terminating EOLs to the files
* [x] Per PEP 257, docstrings must end with periods and one-line docstrings should be on one line (as they are currently), not with `'''"` above and below (I didn't make review comments for this to avoid noise)

Thanks, and looking forward to seeing this in final form!

This is still very WIP.

For today, I have been focusing on what is needed to remove the `CoordinateFrame` methods `coordinates()` and `coordinate_to_quantity` as we should be able to use the Astropy wcsapi infrastructure to do those conversions for us now.

To this end, I started cleaning up the transform APIs (as users of those methods). I have added private `_call_forward` and `_call_backward` methods which only handle the old `with_units=False` case.

There appear to be multiple inconsistencies in how Quantity & high level objects were handled in various places around the API, it seems we were a lot more permissive than the docs stated, and would convert pretty much anything. I have maintained the ability to call the transforms with or without quantity irrespective of if the underlying model uses quantity or not.

After all this I have a question: There is clearly need to keep a more flexible API than the APE 14 API to handle kwargs being passed through to the transform or to control the numerical inverse. **Should we continue to use `with_units=` as a way to differentiate the high and low level object inputs/outputs, or should we have separate methods like APE 14?**

Change of index names should affect all required attributes of the HDK frame (actually, creating a new object with updated attributes). It's incorrect to apply modifications to the index cache only because these changes are going to be lost if you try to apply some other operations other than `to_pandas` or `index` property access. This cache shouldn't be the main source of meta information. And again, HDK frames are supposed to be immutable because they always can be a part of some computation tree.

I agree that using the index cache might be useful in `to_pandas` for performance reasons, so I'd say we can accept these changes but only if we don't say that it resolves `index.names` access issue.

Also, we should update the issue with a new test case to demonstrate that the problem still exists. Otherwise, it might be closed sometime later as not reproducible.

@pytest.mark.asyncio
async def test_git_clone_success():

        with patch("jupyterlab_git.git.execute") as mock_execute:
            # Given
            output = "output"
            mock_execute.return_value = maybe_future((0, output, "error"))
            
            # Create a temporary directory and fake the content of it to be a directory containing a `.git` folder (that should be removed)
            # I advice you to use the pytest fixture tmp_path; see https://docs.pytest.org/en/6.2.x/reference.html#std-fixture-tmp_path

                path="",  # Here set the path to the temporary directory
                repo_url="ghjkhjkl",
                flag=True

            # Then
            mock_execute.assert_called_once_with(
                ["git", "clone", "ghjkhjkl"], # to be update
                cwd=str(Path("/bin") / "test_curr_path"), # to be udpated
                env={"TEST": "test", "GIT_TERMINAL_PROMPT": "0"},
            )
            assert {"code": 0, "message": output} == actual_response
            
            # Check the `.git` folder has been removed.
